{{Chess Opening Theory/Position |Nimzowitsch–Larsen attack |eco=[[Chess/ECOA|A01]] |parent=[[../|Starting position]] }} == 1. b3 · Nimzowitsch–Larsen attack == '''1. b3''' prepares to fianchetto the queen's bishop to b2, where it will fight for the central squares and point towards Black's kingside. This is a [[Wikipedia:Hypermodernism (chess)|hypermodern opening]], preparing to control the centre from afar with a bishop rather than a central pawn (the classical style). This allows Black to seize the centre with a pawn themselves, which they usually choose to do with either 1...e5 or 1...d5. 1...Nf6 is also common. [[/1...e5|'''1...e5''']], the '''modern variation''', is Black's most ambitious and common response to 1. b3, occurring in about 45% of games. Black usually limits the scope of White's bishop and gets a big centre with both d and e pawns after 2. Bb2 Nc6 3. e3 d5. [[/1...d5|'''1...d5''']], the '''classical variation''', is a common sideline (27%). It preserves the option to fianchetto the black bishop to g7 to oppose the white one. White can play 2. Bb2 or 2. Nf3. [[/1...Nf6|'''1...Nf6''']], the '''Indian variation''', prepares to fianchetto the bishop to g7 immediately to oppose the white bishop on b2. However, if Black does play g6 after 2. Bb2, White can play 3. e4!, threatening to kick the knight on f6. 3...Nxe4?? loses to 4. Bxh8. It is an interesting second sideline (15%). ===Minor sidelines=== * [[/1...c5|'''1...c5''']], '''English variation''': Retains option of 1...d5 while also being a good transpositional tool. 2. e4 transposes into the Sicilian defence, and 2. c4 transposes into the symmetrical English. 2. Nf3 is also playable. Occurs about 5% of games. * '''1...b6''', '''Symmetrical variation''': Black copies White's idea. This is perfectly fine for Black. Occurs about 2% of games. * '''1...g6''': Accounts for another 2% of games. Black prepares to fianchetto to g7, though after 2. Bb2 Black must play 2...Nf6 first and this transposes to the Indian variation. There are a number of other "also-ran" sidelines possible that together make up about 5% of competitive level games, including: * '''1...f5''', '''Dutch variation''': This is a rather unexplored alternative. 2. d4 and 2. Nf3 are most commonly played after this, though 2. e3 and even 2. e4!? (borrowing ideas from the Staunton gambit) are both possible. * '''1...e6''': Sets up a variation of the French defence. 2. e4 d5 3. Bb2 is the most common and recommended line. * '''1...c6''': Sets up a variation of the Caro-Kann. 2. e4 d5 3. Bb2 is the most common and recommended line. * '''1...Nc6''': Supports the advance of the e-pawn but allows 2. d4, pressuring the knight. * '''1...b5!?''', '''Polish variation''': A fun alternative to the other moves. Transpositions are now impossible. * '''1...a5?!''': Somewhat playable, trying to ruin White's fianchetto, but seeing that White will likely castle on the kingside, this is not that useful. === History === Also known as '''Nimzowitsch-Larsen attack''' or just '''queen's fianchetto opening''', it is named after its pioneers [[w:Aron Nimzowitsch|Aron Nimzowitsch]] and [[w:Bent Larsen|Bent Larsen]]. Nimzowitsch preferred an alternate move order, [[../1. Nf3|1. Nf3]], and only after 1...d5 committing to 2. b3. This has the advantage of preventing 1...e5 thereby avoiding that line. == Theory table == '''1.b3''' {| border="0" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="4" |- ! ! align="left" | 1 ! align="left" | 2 ! align="left" | 3 ! align="left" | 4 ! align="left" | 5 ! align="left" | 6 ! align="left" | 7 ! align="left" | 8 |- ! align="right" | Classical Variation | ...
